items.span_text: We evaluate 4 closed- and 16 open-source LLMs with MINT. We measure LLMs’ tool-augmented task-solving capability by analyzing their performance from multi-turn tool use (§3.2). items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: To bridge these gaps, we introduce MINT. It is a benchmark for LLMs that measures their performance during multi-turn interaction, focusing on two particular capabilities (§2.1): (1) toolaugmented task-solving; (2) leveraging natural language feedback. MINT mirrors the real-world user-LLM-tool collaborative problem-solving setting. To solve a problem, the LLM can use external tools by generating and executing Python programs (Wang et al., 2024) and/or collecting natural language feedback to refine its solutions; the feedback is provided by GPT-4 (OpenAI, 2023), aiming to simulate human users in a reproducible and scalable way.<sup>2</sup> For a comprehensive evaluation, we include eight established datasets spanning reasoning, code generation, and decision-making (§2.2). To facilitate affordable multi-turn evaluation, after collecting 29,307 diverse instances from existing datasets (Tab. 1), we construct a subset of 586 challenging and representative instances that require multi-turn interaction to solve<sup>3</sup>.
items.title: MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback

items.span_text: Agent consistency via pass^k. As shown in Figure 4, the chance of reliably and consistently solving the same task multiple times significantly drops as the number of trials k increases. Even for the best-performing gpt-4o function calling agent which has a > 60% average task success, pass^8 drop to < 25%. In real-world scenarios, it is important and challenging not just to build agents with high average success (pass^1), but with more robustness and consistency (pass^k trend).

items.span_text: Pass^k metric. For tasks like code generation with good verification techniques (unit tests), the community has defined the pass@k (pass at k) metric as the chance that at least one out of k i.i.d. task trials is successful, which captures the trend of agents enabling discovery of solutions with scaling of inference-time compute [5]. For real-world agent tasks requiring reliability and consistency like customer service, we propose a new metric – pass^k (pass hat k), defined as the chance that all k i.i.d. task trials are successful, averaged across tasks. Therefore, if a task is run for n trials and c of those trials end up successful (r = 1), unbiased estimates for pass^k and pass@k would be:
items.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

items.span_text: We have presented τ -bench, a novel benchmark for evaluating the reliability of agents in interacting with humans and tools in dynamic and realistic settings. The benchmark leverages the latest advances in LMs to simulate users, allows for automated testing of agents and provides an assessment of an agent’s ability to follow domain-specific rules in a consistent manner. Our results show that even SOTA LMs are far from being reliable for use in real-world settings.

Thus, pass^k can capture the reliability of the agent at handling variations in conversations with the same underlying semantics while adhering to the domain policies and rules. final_answer.markdown:
# Are MINT's and tau-bench's Findings Contradictory?

**Short answer: No.** The two findings address different evaluation dimensions and can coexist. They are complementary rather than contradictory.

## What each finding actually measures

**MINT — per-turn gains within a single task instance.**
MINT measures how much performance improves as additional interaction resources are provided *within* a single task run. Its headline result is that "All models benefit from tool interaction and natural language feedback, with absolute performance gains by 1–8% for each additional turn of tool use, and 2–17% with natural language feedback." The unit of analysis is one task; the dependent variable is the gain from each extra turn of tool use / feedback along that single trajectory. [1] MINT frames this as measuring two capabilities: "(1) tool-augmented task-solving; (2) leveraging natural language feedback." [2]

**τ-bench — reliability across independent trials of the same task.**
τ-bench introduces a different metric: "pass^k (pass hat k), defined as the chance that all k i.i.d. task trials are successful, averaged across tasks." [3] Its headline finding is that "the chance of reliably and consistently solving the same task multiple times significantly drops as the number of trials k increases. Even for the best-performing gpt-4o function calling agent which has a > 60% average task success, pass^8 drop to < 25%." [4] The unit of analysis is k independent runs; the dependent variable is the probability that *all* of them succeed.

## Why these are not contradictory

1. **Different axes of evaluation.** MINT's "gain" is a *monotonic, within-instance* effect (each additional turn yields an average improvement). τ-bench's pass^k is a *cross-instance consistency* effect (variance across independent runs). A model can show positive average per-turn gains *and* still have high cross-trial variance — these are independent properties of an agent's behavior.

2. **Different reference points.** MINT's gains are computed relative to the model's earlier-turn performance on the same trajectory. τ-bench's pass^k is computed relative to a fresh, independent trial. A turn-by-turn improvement curve tells you nothing about whether the next *fresh* run will land on the same successful trajectory.

3. **τ-bench itself frames average success and reliability as distinct desiderata.** It explicitly says "it is important and challenging not just to build agents with high average success (pass^1), but with more robustness and consistency (pass^k trend)." [4] By default τ-bench reports "the average reward across tasks, pass^1 = pass@1 = E[r] = E[c/n]" as the main comparison metric, while reserving pass^k for the reliability question. [5] This framing directly accepts that high pass^1 (which is what MINT-style gains contribute to) and high pass^k are separate goals — and that achieving one does not entail the other.

4. **The two findings are mutually reinforcing, not in tension.** τ-bench's conclusion that "even SOTA LMs are far from being reliable for use in real-world settings" [6] is consistent with MINT's broader message that multi-turn / interactive evaluation reveals shortcomings that single-turn metrics hide (e.g., MINT's observation that open-source models still trail closed-source ones even with feedback). Both papers converge on the message that gains in interactive settings exist but are uneven across models and far from sufficient for deployment.

## What the apparent tension actually reflects

The reason the two findings *feel* like they could conflict is that both involve "multi-turn interaction." But MINT's multi-turn axis is *temporal within a task* (turn index → expected gain), while τ-bench's multi-trial axis is *stochastic across re-samples of the same task* (trial index → probability all succeed). They answer different questions:

- MINT asks: "If we let the agent keep going and give it more feedback, does it improve in expectation?"
- τ-bench asks: "If we restart the agent on the same task, will it succeed every time?"

A positive answer to the first question is fully compatible with a negative answer to the second.

## Caveat from the corpus

Within the available papers I did not find any direct cross-citation or explicit joint discussion linking MINT's gain numbers to τ-bench's pass^k numbers on the same model set. The citation graph returned for the two papers in this corpus does not include an edge between them. So the comparison above is a reading of what each paper claims about its own evaluation protocol, not a finding that either paper draws about the other. A head-to-head reconciliation (e.g., the same model evaluated under both protocols and the two metrics compared on the same task) is not directly supported by the corpus and would require additional sources.

Sources
[1] MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback, 1 INTRODUCTION, p. 2
[2] MINT: Evaluating LLMs in Multi-turn Interaction with Tools and Language Feedback, 1 INTRODUCTION, p. 1
[3] tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains, 3 τ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction, p. 4
[4] tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains, 5.1 Main results, p. 7
[5] tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains, 3 τ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction, p. 5
[6] tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains, 6 Discussion, p. 8

locations.preview: Failure breakdown. We sample 115 gpt-4o FC agent trajectories in τ -retail (1 trial per task), out of which 40 tasks have failed (pass^1=65.2%). Upon manual examination of these failures, 4 of them are caused by user instruction typo or ambiguity (and then fixed), and the remaining 36 failure cases are agent issues, which are broken down into more detail below and in Figure 5.
locations.title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

locations.preview: Cost analysis. When we pair gpt-4o FC agent with gpt-4 user simulation on τ -retail, the agent / user simulation costs are \$0.38 / \$0.23 per task respectively, so running one trial per task costs around 200 dollars. For the agent, the input prompt / completion output take up 95.9% / 4.1% of the price respectively, so the cost is mainly due to long system prompt (domain policy + function definitions).

Simplicity is important for the logical consistency of various components and the ease of API and task annotation. Still, a minimally realistic domain requires at least tens of schemas, APIs, rules, and turns out to be challenging enough for existing agents. See § B.1 for more.

arguments.markdown:
# Are MINT's and tau-bench's Findings Contradictory?

**Short answer: No.** The two findings address different evaluation dimensions and can coexist. They are complementary rather than contradictory.

## What each finding actually measures

**MINT — per-turn gains within a single task instance.**
MINT measures how much performance improves as additional interaction resources are provided *within* a single task run. Its headline result is that "All models benefit from tool interaction and natural language feedback, with absolute performance gains by 1–8% for each additional turn of tool use, and 2–17% with natural language feedback." The unit of analysis is one task; the dependent variable is the gain from each extra turn of tool use / feedback along that single trajectory. [[ev_540173e000b7a95c]] MINT frames this as measuring two capabilities: "(1) tool-augmented task-solving; (2) leveraging natural language feedback." [[ev_b802743d249043cc]]

**τ-bench — reliability across independent trials of the same task.**
τ-bench introduces a different metric: "pass^k (pass hat k), defined as the chance that all k i.i.d. task trials are successful, averaged across tasks." [[ev_bee8b0ba211e6933]] Its headline finding is that "the chance of reliably and consistently solving the same task multiple times significantly drops as the number of trials k increases. Even for the best-performing gpt-4o function calling agent which has a > 60% average task success, pass^8 drop to < 25%." [[ev_6186ffb95632dd99]] The unit of analysis is k independent runs; the dependent variable is the probability that *all* of them succeed.

## Why these are not contradictory

1. **Different axes of evaluation.** MINT's "gain" is a *monotonic, within-instance* effect (each additional turn yields an average improvement). τ-bench's pass^k is a *cross-instance consistency* effect (variance across independent runs). A model can show positive average per-turn gains *and* still have high cross-trial variance — these are independent properties of an agent's behavior.

2. **Different reference points.** MINT's gains are computed relative to the model's earlier-turn performance on the same trajectory. τ-bench's pass^k is computed relative to a fresh, independent trial. A turn-by-turn improvement curve tells you nothing about whether the next *fresh* run will land on the same successful trajectory.

3. **τ-bench itself frames average success and reliability as distinct desiderata.** It explicitly says "it is important and challenging not just to build agents with high average success (pass^1), but with more robustness and consistency (pass^k trend)." [[ev_6186ffb95632dd99]] By default τ-bench reports "the average reward across tasks, pass^1 = pass@1 = E[r] = E[c/n]" as the main comparison metric, while reserving pass^k for the reliability question. [[ev_0e982539599ca2b0]] This framing directly accepts that high pass^1 (which is what MINT-style gains contribute to) and high pass^k are separate goals — and that achieving one does not entail the other.

4. **The two findings are mutually reinforcing, not in tension.** τ-bench's conclusion that "even SOTA LMs are far from being reliable for use in real-world settings" [[ev_5fd43d576b0b31f7]] is consistent with MINT's broader message that multi-turn / interactive evaluation reveals shortcomings that single-turn metrics hide (e.g., MINT's observation that open-source models still trail closed-source ones even with feedback). Both papers converge on the message that gains in interactive settings exist but are uneven across models and far from sufficient for deployment.

## What the apparent tension actually reflects

The reason the two findings *feel* like they could conflict is that both involve "multi-turn interaction." But MINT's multi-turn axis is *temporal within a task* (turn index → expected gain), while τ-bench's multi-trial axis is *stochastic across re-samples of the same task* (trial index → probability all succeed). They answer different questions:

- MINT asks: "If we let the agent keep going and give it more feedback, does it improve in expectation?"
- τ-bench asks: "If we restart the agent on the same task, will it succeed every time?"

A positive answer to the first question is fully compatible with a negative answer to the second.

## Caveat from the corpus

Within the available papers I did not find any direct cross-citation or explicit joint discussion linking MINT's gain numbers to τ-bench's pass^k numbers on the same model set. The citation graph returned for the two papers in this corpus does not include an edge between them. So the comparison above is a reading of what each paper claims about its own evaluation protocol, not a finding that either paper draws about the other. A head-to-head reconciliation (e.g., the same model evaluated under both protocols and the two metrics compared on the same task) is not directly supported by the corpus and would require additional sources.

research_answer.markdown:
# Are MINT's and tau-bench's Findings Contradictory?

**Short answer: No.** The two findings address different evaluation dimensions and can coexist. They are complementary rather than contradictory.

## What each finding actually measures

**MINT — per-turn gains within a single task instance.**
MINT measures how much performance improves as additional interaction resources are provided *within* a single task run. Its headline result is that "All models benefit from tool interaction and natural language feedback, with absolute performance gains by 1–8% for each additional turn of tool use, and 2–17% with natural language feedback." The unit of analysis is one task; the dependent variable is the gain from each extra turn of tool use / feedback along that single trajectory. [1] MINT frames this as measuring two capabilities: "(1) tool-augmented task-solving; (2) leveraging natural language feedback." [2]

**τ-bench — reliability across independent trials of the same task.**
τ-bench introduces a different metric: "pass^k (pass hat k), defined as the chance that all k i.i.d. task trials are successful, averaged across tasks." [3] Its headline finding is that "the chance of reliably and consistently solving the same task multiple times significantly drops as the number of trials k increases. Even for the best-performing gpt-4o function calling agent which has a > 60% average task success, pass^8 drop to < 25%." [4] The unit of analysis is k independent runs; the dependent variable is the probability that *all* of them succeed.

## Why these are not contradictory

1. **Different axes of evaluation.** MINT's "gain" is a *monotonic, within-instance* effect (each additional turn yields an average improvement). τ-bench's pass^k is a *cross-instance consistency* effect (variance across independent runs). A model can show positive average per-turn gains *and* still have high cross-trial variance — these are independent properties of an agent's behavior.

2. **Different reference points.** MINT's gains are computed relative to the model's earlier-turn performance on the same trajectory. τ-bench's pass^k is computed relative to a fresh, independent trial. A turn-by-turn improvement curve tells you nothing about whether the next *fresh* run will land on the same successful trajectory.

3. **τ-bench itself frames average success and reliability as distinct desiderata.** It explicitly says "it is important and challenging not just to build agents with high average success (pass^1), but with more robustness and consistency (pass^k trend)." [4] By default τ-bench reports "the average reward across tasks, pass^1 = pass@1 = E[r] = E[c/n]" as the main comparison metric, while reserving pass^k for the reliability question. [5] This framing directly accepts that high pass^1 (which is what MINT-style gains contribute to) and high pass^k are separate goals — and that achieving one does not entail the other.

4. **The two findings are mutually reinforcing, not in tension.** τ-bench's conclusion that "even SOTA LMs are far from being reliable for use in real-world settings" [6] is consistent with MINT's broader message that multi-turn / interactive evaluation reveals shortcomings that single-turn metrics hide (e.g., MINT's observation that open-source models still trail closed-source ones even with feedback). Both papers converge on the message that gains in interactive settings exist but are uneven across models and far from sufficient for deployment.

## What the apparent tension actually reflects

The reason the two findings *feel* like they could conflict is that both involve "multi-turn interaction." But MINT's multi-turn axis is *temporal within a task* (turn index → expected gain), while τ-bench's multi-trial axis is *stochastic across re-samples of the same task* (trial index → probability all succeed). They answer different questions:

- MINT asks: "If we let the agent keep going and give it more feedback, does it improve in expectation?"
- τ-bench asks: "If we restart the agent on the same task, will it succeed every time?"

A positive answer to the first question is fully compatible with a negative answer to the second.

## Caveat from the corpus

Within the available papers I did not find any direct cross-citation or explicit joint discussion linking MINT's gain numbers to τ-bench's pass^k numbers on the same model set. The citation graph returned for the two papers in this corpus does not include an edge between them. So the comparison above is a reading of what each paper claims about its own evaluation protocol, not a finding that either paper draws about the other. A head-to-head reconciliation (e.g., the same model evaluated under both protocols and the two metrics compared on the same task) is not directly supported by the corpus and would require additional sources.

Sources

[4] tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains, 5.1 Main results, p. 7
